speaker: interviewer
text: Today is February 14, 1997. I am interviewing a survivor, Lisbeth Kornreich. My name is Rae Meltzer. And we are doing the interview in Bethesda, Maryland, in the USA, conducting in the English language. My name is Rae Meltzer. Today is February 14, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Lisbeth Kornreich in Bethesda, Maryland, USA. Please give your name and spell it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Lisbeth Kornreich, L-I-S-B-E-T-H K-O-R-N-R-E-I-C-H. My maiden name is Modley. I just retained the M as a middle initial-- M-O-D-L-E-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Lisbeth Ida Modley, Ida after my late grandmother on my father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-D-A-- Lisbeth, L-I-S-B-E-T-H I-D-A and then M-O-D-L-Y

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other names that you used?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't, except that the short version for Lisbeth from where I, where I come from is Liesl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I spell that L-I-E-S-L. But you can-- some Liesls spell it without the E or any other way. This is the way I spell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born May 14, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will be 84 in a couple of months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And please spell the city and country of your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vienna, V-I-E-N-N-A, in Austria, A-U-S-T-R-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, thank you. Now we'll begin the interview. And I wonder if you would start by talking about what you remember of your childhood in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was an only child. And I was my grandfather's first grandchild. When I say grandfather, that means I never knew my grandmothers. One died a few months before my birth, the other one a few months after my birth. And my paternal grandfather had died before my father was an adult. So I had one doting grandpa. My mother was his favorite. And I was, of course, spoiled. And I enjoyed that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lived in a fairly small apartment, by the standards that we later were able to reach. And, as I said, there were no other children. My mother's two sisters, my aunts, were just wonderful. The war broke out, as you might remember, in August, 1914. 1915, my father was drafted. And so I grew-- I remember my early childhood without men around because we didn't talk to strangers. There were very few men in the family. It was a small family. So it was my grandpa, my mother, and my two aunts. And they all played with me, talked to me, sang to me, took me for walks, made pretty birthday parties. I do remember my 4th birthday very vividly because one aunt-- and that was my father's sister, who passed away still during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is a very far away memory of her, lady with a high collar. And I got a beautiful winter coat that I thought was lined in gold like all these fairy tales. Actually, that was some kind of a yellow lining fabric. But I do remember that birthday when I got the coat lined in gold. So your childhood was-- you were surrounded by adults. Did you have opportunities to play with young children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, there-- either there were none or they might carry germs or stuff like that. And in my early childhood, I was not given an opportunity to play with children. Then I went to school when I was six. And that was right after the war when the big Austro-Hungarian empire shrank to the little Republic of Austria that I know as the country where I grew up. And many refugees-- they all came to Vienna, which had been their capital. And there wasn't enough housing. And then I really caught every germ that went around so that after a few months where I was more sick than well-- and a very fast-growing, very thin child. And food wasn't always available. And these were bad times.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents took me out of school and had me tutored, which means a lady came one hour a day. And then at the end of the school year, I had to pass an exam in the regular public school to be advanced to the next level. And I was such a brilliant grade school student that I was told to skip the last year. So I was always among the youngest one when I went to high school. Tell us about your schooling.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- well, by then-- when I was about 10, which would be '23-- by then, of course, things had settled down. My father had gotten in a position where he could afford to send me to a private school. And there was a school which had originally been founded by a very forward and progressive-looking parents to give girls a chance to pursue an academic career, which meant at age 10, it was decided, you are going to go to the university.

speaker: interviewer
text: At 10, you were told?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if you want to go to public schools, that's another story. But if you want to enter this type of what was called gymnasium, which has nothing to do with gymnasium, or realschule, which was more science-oriented, then you were destined, hopefully, to become a professional. Now, girls in those days were only given free spots in boys' schools. If there were two vacancies, two girls could come in. Next year, another family moved into the area. And there was only one, one vacancy. So one girl had to stay home. That was before my generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that is why this school and two or three others were established to give girls an uninterrupted academic schooling. And so I got into a realgymnasium, combined science with languages, a combination of realschule and gymnasium. And-- Would you tell us the name of the school and how you might spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the [? Madchen-- ?] Girls' Realgymnasium, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? Albertgasse-- ?] [? Madchen ?] Realgymnasium Acht. It was the 8th district. And what I ever became-- I don't mean professionally, but as a person-- I owe, first of all, to my parents, but then to a large extent to this magnificent group of women who were knowledgeable and capable and sort of maybe this-- if I could compare that-- the suffragette generation. We were instilled with a great sense of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] perfectionism. Women can do anything. And they can do it better. But they have to apply themselves. When I think of today's schools here, this, this lack of motivation and whatnot, that didn't exist. It was very easy to be told not to come back. And the-- from-- there were eight grades. And from grade one to grade eight-- well, if I tell you we were 17 when we graduated in my class, which was one section. The other section had some 20-odd girls. So I think we were under 50, or not much over 40, when we graduated that year in 1939. But we had a wonderful time. It was not all, all studies. We had extracurricular activities like lab work on a voluntary basis. We also had tennis. And-- that was in the afternoon, after school let out after three. And of course, we had school on Saturday. That was every school still had a half day in those days. And we had eight years of Latin and five years of English and-- British English-- and lots of physics and chemistry, for which I was not very well-suited. But you know, you put your nose in the book. And there were no excuses. And I graduated with fairly good marks, if you want to see my [GERMAN]. It, it is something like the baccalaureate program internationally. And you do get credit at American colleges and universities for about one or two years, depending, having graduated from a realgymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this school financially supported or sponsored by an organization of some kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, the school was started as a private school. Sure, there was tuition to be paid. Tuition was paid all along. But each year, the state licensed one grade. And so when I graduated, we were completely state-licensed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does that mean they gave financial support as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that means that they, that they accepted the grades and the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The licenses, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, as you were growing up and going to school, did your contact, your environment, change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we by then, moved into a very large apartment and later on into a private villa. And my parents were always very cultured people. And by then, they could afford to buy paintings and oriental rugs and whatnot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I grew up in a very, for that time, luxurious home. And then the Depression hit. And, and-- but this was there. I mean, the good furniture was there. The Biedermeier furniture and the rugs were there. And though the circumstances in the country became very, very pitiful, I personally was still a very sheltered person in one sense.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the other hand, of course, I was aware of what goes on. Because if you see these long lines of the unemployed, and, and when you see the homeless, and when you see all this desperation, you're not blind to it. And that social consciousness was always with my family and with me. And-- but it taught me to appreciate the good things in life so much more. What you might-- what some might consider basics, I realized was really not so basic. This was not the given that you had a warm place and, and, and food on the table. And though I was never politically active and neither were my parents, we were always very aware of what went on and, and politically knowledgeable, to a certain extent. And then, of course, I formed these wonderful friendships in, in school. I had always wanted sisters and brothers and uncles and aunts and cousins. Of course, I had uncles and aunts. And the cousins were-- I had two male cousins 10, 14 years older than myself. And, and then I had, on my mother's side, a 10-year-younger girl cousin. But there was no age group that I really belonged to until I got to school and, and had this chance to meet children from intellectual houses and homes. And there are friendships that I treasured. And of course, at my age, there are not many left. But I still have a childhood friend who now lives in Florida. And we are like sisters. She also was an only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the children who came to this school, were they from different religious backgrounds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Austria was a Catholic country. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] most, or many of them, were Catholic. But then this gave us Jewish students-- and there was a large contingent of Jewish girls and two or three Protestants-- Protestants were not very numerous in Austria in those days-- a chance to get our religious education as part of the school curriculum. Because the Holy See had a, a pact or whatever you call it with the Austrian government that religion has to be taught during school hours, meaning Catholic religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what we are going to do with the others? So a rabbi was brought in twice a week, an hour at a time, to teach us the basics. And a priest was brought in for the two or three Protestant students. And this was part-- you'll find religion as one of the subjects in which we were graded. That was part of the general school program. And you left that school in '39, did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no, no, no, no. I graduated in 1931.

speaker: interviewee
text: At age 18 and went to the university. Those were relatively quiet days. They were actually very-- years-- and went on to study philosophy at the main--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which university was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: University of Vienna, Wien Universitat, which is some 700 years old and very prestigious. And I worked in the laboratories, studying chemistry, for which I was not very well-suited. And, in retrospect, I must say fortunately, I was sickly at that time. I was a big, strong girl. But I got all kinds of maladies. And so I had to stop. And the colleagues from my first semester, of course, they went on and on. And by the time I got back, they were up there. And I felt very left out. And I was also not very ambitious. And I finally was able to convince my father that maybe that was not the career for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had registered long enough to get a piece of paper, which, in Austria, would be meaningless. But I figured any piece of paper would be valuable when I come to the United States. So in '39, I went back and got the Absolutorium. All it says-- all it means was that you have registered long enough to graduate if you had done your exams and had written your thesis and had finished your studies. But that, it doesn't say on the paper. So I can show you, Absolutorium from the Vienna University.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds impressive-- absolute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, the impressive part of it is-- impressive, nothing's, you know-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A, I'm a lucky person. And I count on that. And B, I had a lot of guts. And C, do I try to be an honest and a truthful person? Sometimes, to save your life, you got to be less than truthful. And what happened was this. I knew that I need-- I thought I needed this piece of paper. So went to the university in 1939. Hitler came in '38, 'Juden [? eintritt ?] verboten.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took the chance. That's where the guts came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went up to the registrar. And I said, I would like to get that Absolutorium. And the woman there said, can you assure me that you are racially pure? And I said, yes, I can. Now, that's not a lie. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's not a lie. It's not what she meant. But that's something else again. And so I got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had your wits about you.

speaker: interviewee
text: If that's what it is, well, I needed that a number of times, yeah. But in an illegal situation, in a, in a Nazi regime, you know, honesty and decency and truthfulness was not always the way to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what happened in your family and to you in 1938 when the Nazis came into Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The weeks before, there was a-- there was great agitation and great activity to get everybody out for a plebiscite, that Austria should remain independent. At that time, we grew-- I grew up in a parliamentary democracy and, and was taught and primed for it. But of course, it didn't work that way. And the parliament was sent home in-- somewhere in the '30s. I forgot. And we had-- first, we had Dollfuss, who was murdered, and then came Schuschnigg as a chancellor without parliament. And so this plebiscite was, was scheduled to take place on Sunday. And we young people, and we young students were-- and we were all so patriotic. And we would walk down the streets and would call out, Osterreich, Osterreich! And on the other side of the street, all very well-organized, on the other side of the street, an equally large group would walk down and say, Heil, Hitler! Heil, Hitler! Osterreich! Heil, Hitler! Osterreich! So I don't have to tell you which side of the street I was on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Friday night that week, before the Sunday, a group of us young people were going to get together and, and, and spend a pleasant evening together and meet at 7:00 or 7:30. I forgot. So around 6 o'clock, one of my girlfriends-- with whom I would have traveled together on the trolley but she lived in my neighborhood so we were going to go together to where we were supposed to meet in a restaurant-- called me and said, are you listening to the radio? I don't think we are going to go out tonight. And why shouldn't we go out tonight? Well, listen to the radio. Well, Hitler was at the far western end of Austria, you know, Tyrol, which borders on Bavaria. And at 7 o'clock, Schuschnigg came on the radio and said, to avoid bloodshed, I have ordered not to resist the German invasion, or whatever he called it, which it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we never did get to meet at that restaurant. And that was-- then Hitler had his triumphal progression from one end of Austria until he got to Vienna. There never was a plebiscite. And, and that, that was that. Now that Friday night when I couldn't go out and my parents, of course, were frightened and dumbfounded and whatnot-- and when you're really frightened, you tend to do insignificant things, dumb things. I've seen people running back into burning houses literally to bring out a jar of preserves, you know, I mean, you sometimes lose your sense of priorities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- we had lots of books and bookcases and whatnot. So we had to go through all the books to make sure we didn't have Karl Marx, or whatever, that could get us into trouble. We didn't think of hiding some money, some jewelery, or God knows what. I still don't know what I would do, probably just sit back and say, this is fate. So we ransacked the library. And then my parents, as I said, they were collectors of art pieces. We had these vitrines, these glass cabinets, with delicate things in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother had, among other things, something 17th, 18th century, 18th century, 19th century, little silver revolver which had no bullets and couldn't shoot. But it was the handiwork, the, the engraving that was so precious. The whole thing was maybe four, five inches long. So with Karl Marx and some other things and that silver revolver, we were going to get rid of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by then, it was night. And my mother had the very wide coat. What did they call it? Steamer coats or travel coats, you know, one of those voluminous, very elegant coats.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we decided under this coat, you could hide anything. And we are going to go to a park and get rid of this. And so Mama and I traipsed out in the middle of the night to a park. And under the big coat was the package.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you dispose of these things? You don't drop it. You don't leave it on the bench. You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moonlight, somebody might see you. There is a shadow. Maybe there's somebody there. Maybe the Nazis are already behind the bushes, God knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walked around for at least an hour, if not more. And then finally, we found a trash bin which was well hidden in the shadows behind the bushes. And in went that little silver revolver and Karl Marx and a few other books.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went home. And we felt very safe now. I mean, all the incriminating things had been taken care of. Next morning, my father wanted to go to the office. And we came to the garage. We had a large car. And-- no garage in the house. The car was garaged about two blocks away. No car. The Obersturmfuhrer da, da, da, da is now driving the car.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the first inkling that this is serious. And then my father wanted to get on the trolley. And of course, he didn't know how much the car fare was, because he hadn't been on a public transportation in a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he wore his rosette that he was a war veteran. Somebody pushed him off the trolley backwards and said, you are not a veteran. You're just nothing but a dirty Jew. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we knew that Hitler was here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Let us stop here for a change of tapes.
